text: The manuscript describes a technique for robotic-assisted pancreatic duodenectomy. The represented patient is a forty-two-year-old female with a prior history of IPMN who presented with an acute onset of pancreatitis. Diagnostic imaging including CT scan and endoscopic ultrasound revealed a complex septate of heterogeneous mass measuring three point three centimeters. Biochemical workup was normal. She was recommended for robotic RAPD.

The patient is positioned supine on a split leg table with the right arm tucked and pressure points padded. The operating table is rotated 45 degrees to facilitate docking of the SI robot from the side.

The robotic and assistant ports are positioned as per the surgical plan, with a liver retractor inserted through the leftmost lateral port. The retractor elevates the gallbladder and lifts the liver superiorly to facilitate the resection process.

The surgeon initiates the resection phase by entering the lesser sac through the greater omentum below the gastroepiploic pedicle. The assistant provides gentle caudal counter-retraction as the dissection progresses along the greater curvature towards the pylorus. The colon is fully mobilized off the duodenum, with the gastroepiploic pedicle preserved and not transected.

The surgeon performs cokerization of the duodenum, grasping and transecting its lateral fibers. The bedside assistant provides medial counter-retraction, facilitating mobilization of the duodenum's third and fourth portions towards the ligament on the right. Dynamic anterior and cranial retraction with A-III exposes the inferior vena cava, left renal vein insertion, and the aorta.

The surgeon completes the release of the ligament of Treitz, exposing the proximal jejunum. The jejunum is extracted through the ligament defect into the right superior colic upper quadrant. The jejunum is measured approximately ten centimeters distal to the ligament, and the mesenteric vessels are sealed using a vessel sealing device.

The surgeon uses a linear vascular stapler to transect the jejunum. Sequential ligation of the mesenteric vessels of the proximal jejunum is performed up to the uncinate process. The pars flaccida is opened carefully to avoid injury to the aberrant left hepatic artery. The stomach is transected approximately five centimeters proximal to the pylorus, with the right gastroepiploic vessels ligated using a vessel sealer device. A thick linear stapler load is used for the stomach transection.

The surgeon ligates the right gastric artery near its origin from the proper hepatic artery using laparoscopic titanium clips and a blunt tip vessel sealing device. The distal gastric staple line is grasped and retracted laterally and inferiorly, placing tension on the common hepatic artery and porta hepatis. Dissection proceeds along the superior border of the pancreas into the porta hepatis, where the common hepatic artery lymph node is identified, resected, and retrieved with a laparoscopic specimen retrieval bag for pathologic analysis. The gastroduodenal artery is fully visualized.

The surgeon identifies the gastroduodenal artery at its takeoff from the common hepatic artery. Using a robotic hook, the artery is circumferentially dissected. A vessel loop is placed around the gastroduodenal artery, which is then transected with a vascular stapler.

The surgeon reinforces the proximal stump with laparoscopic titanium vascular clips. The portal vein is dissected in a cephalad direction for two to three centimeters. The plane between the common bile duct and the portal vein is identified and developed posteriorly. The common bile duct is transected with a 60mm curved tip vascular linear stapler above the biliary stent. The lateral border of the portal vein is further dissected using robotic hook cautery.

The surgeon ligates the superior pancreatic duodenal artery using a blunt tip vessel sealing device. Dissection of the portal vein progresses from superior to inferior, reaching the superior border of the pancreas and exposing the superior tunnel. A robotic arm retracts the distal gastric staple line laterally and cephalad, stretching the gastroepiploic vein as it enters the anterior superior mesenteric vein (SMV).

The surgeon opens the fatty tissue near the pancreatic inferior border using electrocautery, revealing the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). The assistant performs gentle dissection to create the inferior tunnel. The right gastroepiploic vein is traced to its insertion in the SMV, dissected, looped with a vessel loop, and ligated using a blunt tip vessel sealing device.

A-III retracts the specimen at the distal gastric staple line laterally, stretching the pancreatic neck. The surgeon transects the pancreatic parenchyma using monopolar curved scissors with electrocautery, carefully identifying the main duct. The assistant lifts the pancreas anteriorly off the SMV during the transection. The main pancreatic duct is transected using scissors without electrocautery, and a 4-5 French pancreatic duct stent is placed into the main duct for identification.

The surgeon transects the remaining pancreatic parenchyma using electrocautery, performing slow and meticulous dissection to prevent significant hemorrhage. A-III provides superior lateral retraction of the specimen, dynamically adjusting its placement throughout the dissection of the pancreatic head and uncinate process.

The surgeon dissects the uncinate process, which consists of three distinct layers. The first layer, containing filamentous fibers between the SMV, portal vein, and pancreatic head, is dissected using hook cautery. The second layer, comprising the first jejunal vein, vein of Belcher, and uncinate branches, is dissected and ligated using A-one and the assistant's blunt tip vessel sealing device. The third layer involves the SMA retroperitoneal margin, with the SMV and portal vein rotated medially using A-two, and ligation performed with the vessel sealing device.

The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunal anastomosis using a two-layer end-to-side duct-to-mucosa technique. A-three is used to retract the sutures cranially for better exposure. Silk transpancreatic horizontal mattress sutures are placed to secure the pancreatic parenchyma to the jejunum, with three sutures positioned above, below, and straddling the main pancreatic duct. The sutures are tied carefully to avoid ductal ligation, and a pancreatic duct stent is used to check duct patency. A-one is switched to monopolar scissors for the aneurotomy.

The surgeon uses a needle driver to place interrupted 5-0 PDS sutures, approximating the jejunal mucosa to the pancreatic duct. At least six sutures are placed, with additional sutures used if the duct size is larger.

The surgeon feeds the pancreatic duct stent through the aneurotomy before placing anterior 5-0 PDS sutures. After completing the anastomosis, the previously used silk sutures for the posterior layer are reused for the anterior layer of the pancreaticojejunostomy.

The surgeon performs a hepatical jejunosomy approximately ten centimeters distal to the pancreatic jejunosomy, using a single-layer anastomosis technique. Monopolar curved scissors are employed to transect the common bile duct staple line, while bowel grasping forceps bring the jejunum closer to the common bile duct. The aneurotomy is made with monopolar curved scissors, and de-anastomosis is completed using interrupted five-o PDA sutures for ducts less than one centimeter in diameter, or two running four-o barb sutures for larger ducts.

The surgeon places and ties posterior sutures for an interrupted anastomosis, using a four to five French biliary stent to maintain patency for ducts less than one centimeter. Additional five-oh PDS sutures are placed to complete the anterior anastomosis, and all sutures are tied to finalize the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy, creating an anticholic, isoperistaltic anastomosis. Two 3-0 silk marking stitches are placed on the jejunum, approximately 40 to 60 centimeters distal to the hepatic jejunostomy, marking the afferent and efferent limbs. The laparoscopic assistant reflects the omentum and mesocolon cephalad, allowing the surgeon to locate the neoduodenum. The distal jejunum is reduced and placed back into the infracolic compartment, then brought in an anticholic isoperistaltic fashion up to the stomach. Large dual-functioning needle drivers are used to place interrupted outer layer sutures with 2-0 silk. The most cephalic suture is held by A-three for retraction, while A-one is replaced with monopolar curved scissors to transect the gastric staple line.

The surgeon uses scissors to create an enterotomy in the jejunum. The anastomosis is performed with two 3-0 barbed sutures in a running canal fashion. An interrupted outer layer with 2-0 silk sutures is placed to complete the gastrojejunostomy.

A 19 French round channel drain is placed anterior to the pancreatic jejunostomy and hepatic jejunostomy. The falciform ligament flap is used to cover the gastroduodenal artery stump. Instruments are removed, the robotic system is undocked, and the fascia and incisions are closed in layers.

The total operative time was two hundred twenty five minutes. Estimated blood loss was twenty five mLs. There was no post operative complications. The final pathology revealed invasive moderately differentiated endocarcinoma arising from branch duct IPMN.
The manuscript provides the detailed steps of a robotic assisted pancreatic duodenectomy as performed at the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center.
